payload: {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/pulls/comments/1893883104","pull_request_review_id":2517456479,"id":1893883104,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABZIq85w4lzg","diff_hunk":"@@ -3,101 +3,148 @@ title: Installation sidebar: false --- -Installations methods include: +The recommended method of installing SciPy depends on your preferred workflow. +The common workflows can roughly be broken down into the following +categories: -- [Distributions](#distributions) -- [pip](#pip-install) -- [conda](#pip-install) -- [Package Manager](#package_manager) -- [Source](#source) +- [Environment-based (e.g. `pip`, `conda`)](#environment-based) (the traditional workflow) +- [Project-based (e.g. `uv`, `pixi`)](#project-based) (recommended for new users) +- [System package managers](#system-package-managers) (simplest) +- [Building from source](#building-from-source) (for debugging and development) -Methods differ in ease of use, coverage, maintenance of old versions, -system-wide versus local environment use, and control. With pip or -Anaconda\'s conda, you can control the package versions for a specific -project to prevent conflicts. Conda also controls non-Python packages, -like MKL or HDF5. System package managers, like `apt-get`, install -across the entire computer, often have older versions, and don\'t have -as many available versions. Source compilation is much more difficult -but is necessary for debugging and development. If you don\'t know which -installation method you need or prefer, we recommend the Scientific -Python Distribution [Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/). +To install SciPy with [static type stubs], +see [Installing with type stubs](#type-stubs). - +[static type stubs]: https://typing.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guides/libraries.html -# Scientific Python Distributions (recommended) + -Python distributions provide the language itself, along with the most -commonly used packages and tools. These downloadable files require -little configuration, work on almost all setups, and provide all the -commonly used scientific Python tools. +## Environment-based workflows -[Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/) works on Windows, Mac, -and Linux, provides over 1,500 Python packages, and is used by over 15 -million people. Anaconda is best suited to beginning users; it provides -a large collection of libraries all in one. +In environment-based workflows, you install packages into an environment, which you +can activate and deactivate. -For more advanced users who will need to install or upgrade regularly, -[Mambaforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge#mambaforge) is a more -suitable way to install the `conda` (and `mamba`, a faster `conda` alternative) -package manager. +If you have installed Python, you can create a virtual environment with a tool such +as `venv` - see [the tutorial in the Python Packaging User Guide][venv-guide]. +You can then install [SciPy from PyPI], the Python Package Index, with [`pip`]: -Other options include: + python -m pip install scipy -- [WinPython](https://winpython.github.io): Another free distribution - including scientific packages and the Spyder IDE; Windows only, but - more actively maintained and supports the latest Python 3 versions. -- [Pyzo](https://pyzo.org): A free distribution based on Anaconda - and the IEP interactive development environment; Supports Linux, - Windows, and Mac. +[venv-guide]: https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/tutorials/installing-packages/#creating-virtual-environments +[SciPy from PyPI]: https://pypi.org/project/scipy/ +[`pip`]: https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/getting-started/ - +Alternatively, you can install [SciPy from conda-forge] using a [Conda]-based +environment manager. You can install Python itself, as well as packages in +languages other than Python, as Conda packages. -# Installing with Pip +[Miniforge] is the recommended way to install `conda` and [`mamba`], +two Conda-based environment managers. You can then install SciPy as follows: -You can install SciPy from PyPI with `pip`: + conda install scipy # or + mamba install scipy - python -m pip install scipy +[SciPy from conda-forge]: https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/scipy +[Miniforge]: https://conda-forge.org/download/ +[Conda]: https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/index.html +[`mamba`]: https://mamba.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ + + + +## Project-based workflows + +In project-based workflows, a project is a directory containing a manifest +file describing the project, a lock-file describing the exact dependencies +of the project, and the project's (potentially multiple) environments. +These workflows provide reproducibility benefits over environment-based workflows. + +You can add [SciPy from PyPI] to a [`uv`] project: + + uv add scipy + +You can also install Python itself with `uv`. + +[`uv`]: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/ + +If you work with non-Python packages, you may prefer to install SciPy as +a Conda package, so that you can use the same workflow for packages which +are not available on PyPI. - +You can add [SciPy from conda-forge] to a [`pixi`] project: -# Installing via Conda + pixi add scipy -You can install SciPy from the `defaults` or `conda-forge` channels with `conda`: +Pixi can also install packages from PyPI, using `uv` under the hood. - conda install scipy +[`pixi`]: https://pixi.sh/latest/ - + -# Install system-wide via a package manager +## Installing system-wide via a system package manager System package managers can install the most common Python packages. They install packages for the entire computer, often use older versions, -and don't have as many available versions. +and don't have as many available versions. They are not the recommended +installation method, but they are the simplest. -## Ubuntu and Debian +### Ubuntu and Debian Using `apt-get`: sudo apt-get install python3-scipy -## Fedora +### Fedora Using `dnf`: sudo dnf install python3-scipy -## macOS +### macOS macOS doesn't have a preinstalled package manager, but you can install [Homebrew](https://brew.sh/) and use it to install SciPy (and Python itself): brew install cc @rgommers @stefanv @jarrodmillman @tupui @jorenham ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/issues/595/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":3,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/issues/595/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/issues/comments/2557004580","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy.org/pull/595#issuecomment-2557004580","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/issues/595","id":2557004580,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIq86YaMsk","user":{"login":"matthew-brett","id":67612,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY3NjEy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/67612?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett","html_url":"https://github.com/matthew-brett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-20T13:22:00Z","updated_at":"2024-12-20T13:22:00Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I must say I am also worried the "project" workflow as the default *for beginners*.

The problem with the project workflow is that the person using the workflow really does have to understand "environments", and this is very advanced for a beginner.

I say that as someone who has been teaching Python to beginners for a long time, to a lot of people.

A beginner may start off with a hazy understanding of a filesystem, let alone the structure of a Python environment - and I'm speaking from experience teaching Berkeley statistics students - and lots of other, less experienced students.

In a recent third-year undergraduate data science class, in a group very used to the Python data science stack and notebooks, we accidentally ran into `pyenv` and caused substantial and long-lasting confusion - because they had no model of what a Python environment was.  My appeal here is to consider the possibility that environments are more advanced and more difficult than we (experienced developers) might think.  And for that reason, I would personally only suggest using `pixi` or `uv` for experienced developers - for whom they are very useful tools.   We should surely point experienced developers to those tools and it would be perfectly reasonable to tell beginners - you should look into environments as you become more experienced, because they are useful in various ways.

For example, look at the second hit for "scipy tutorial" on Google just now:

https://www.w3schools.com/python/scipy/index.php

It has the following first example:

```
from scipy import constants

print(constants.liter) 
```

I'm sure we'd all agree that we want to get the beginner to that state, where they are concentrating on using Scipy, as quickly and easily as possible - and in particular, if possible - without needing to understand (I would argue) difficult concepts like Python environments.

For example - I just ran the Python.org GUI installer on Mac, following all the defaults.   I then open Terminal and:

```
# Once only, won't need to think about this again.
pip3 install scipy ipython
ipython
```

- at which point I can copy / paste the example above, and it works.

If I close the terminal, and open it again, I don't have to activate anything, or be in a particular directory, I can just do:

```bash
ipython
```

and start copy-pasting.   It seems to me this is substantially easier for a beginner, such as a science undergraduate, than the per-project (`pixi`) or virtualenv-based workflows - and will better match much of the information already out there.

As long as we say - look, you'll probably need this environment stuff later - we will not hold them back as they get 6 or 12 months into their scientific Python journey.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/issues/comments/2557004580/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

> 
> I'd much prefer to keep pointing to Anaconda then if there must be a "no environments nor project managers" level, it's way better than python.org installers.

I'm proposing that Python.org installers are totally fine for beginner science undergraduates - maybe we should discuss that if you think that isn't true?  But really - any methods (except Anaconda - due the Terms of Service) that don't require the user to think about or interact with environments would be OK.   Unless I've misunderstood, I think that excludes `uv` and `pixi`.

> > very rarely leads to any problems for the beginner
> 
> This is not true I think beyond the duration of perhaps a single initial course with a limited set of packages. It works okay for a while, but using `pip install` repeatedly inevitably degrades an environment over time. And once it breaks, it's a huge pain for the user to understand what is going on or to fix it.

I can tell you that I have taught many students now, and problems like that are rare, and generally easy to fix with the proposed methods that come up on StackOverflow.   My students for example, start off with the instructions:

```
pip3 install scipy matplotlib pandas statsmodels scikit-learn seaborn ipython jupyter
```

and generally need very little else for a year or two.  By which time time they are in a much better position to adopt virtualenv workflows.

Do you think it isn't true that beginners will find environments like `pixi` confusing?  Would it help if I did a walkthrough to show where they are likely to get confused?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/issues/comments/2557110547/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

> 
> How about we just stick with JupyterLite then? Do we need a level between that and environments?

As I was saying above - it seems overly strict to insist that people stick with JupyterLite or go straight to understanding environments.  JupyterLite has some serious downsides, including the difficulty of installing new packages, and the fact that everythings sits in the browser cache. in fact I've been in that discussion over at [astral-sh/uv#2077](https://github.com/astral-sh/uv/issues/2077) - could you say more how one would do that?
> 
> Depends what you want to do. `uv run --with scipy example.py` is quite simple. Their guides are pretty straightforward even for beginner https://docs.astral.sh/uv/guides/ if you are used to dancing with envs this is actually harder to wrap your head around all that because its a different take and vision.

The use case is the most basic one possible - how do I get up and running with a Python environment that includes Scipy.   For the moment, I have no need for a Python environment that doesn't include Scipy - or any other environment.  When I install a package, I want to be able to import it without further ado from my terminal IPython or Jupyter.

This is the model that most tutorial out there will use.

With Python.org I run my basic install command, e.g.:

```bash
pip3 install scipy ipython
```

and from then on, I can `import scipy`.  

However `uv run --with scipy example.py` prompts the following questions:

* What is this `uv run` command?  Why isn't the command `python3` as it is for most other tutorials?
*  Why do I have to specify all my dependencies on the command line there?   Why can't I just install `scipy` and be done with it?  I'm going to keep using Scipy for everything.

So - yes - sure - if you really need separable isolated environments - `uv` or `pipx`.  If not - which is the case for scientists who aren't experienced coders, and may well never be experienced coders - then give them the simplest possible thing, that will match what they see out in the interwebs.

It would satisfy my concerns if the changes just reversed the order (from beginner to advanced) of the `uv`/`pixi` option with the `pip` / `conda` option.

For the Python.org installers - is this due to the fact that environments can get stale and inconsistent over time?   Is this really a veto for their use in simple cases - when that problem is rare?   At least, shouldn't we discuss / assess whether it is rare or not, rather than assuming it?   Our experience as developers will be very different from that of beginners. 

It seems to me this is a practicality over purity issue, where we should prioritize practicality for our beginner users, while acknowledging that more advanced users will want something that can protect themselves from those problems, partly because the cognitive load of using environments is less for those users, and partly because advanced users do much more complex and frequent installs.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/issues/comments/2557370567/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

But... you could provide a more tool agnostic guide by having a blurb of line metadata someone can copy into a script?


> > the proposal simply to relabel the pip way as the beginner way?
> 
> this
> 
> > is this due to the fact that environments can get stale and inconsistent over time? Is this really a veto for their use in simple cases - when that problem is rare?
> 
> it is absolutely not rare, it is quite common. there's a whole XKCD comic about it and uncountable issues, SO posts, etc.

I think it is rare - for beginners - because I've been there when lots of beginners have done lots of installs.    So, even if there is an XKCD comic on it (didn't that include `easy_install`?) I don't think that question is outside the range of reasonable discussion.

> I can't spend the time digging up the whole history here, but we had a very very long discussion across multiple projects about recommending Anaconda distribution vs. python.org+pip, which I'm sure you remember (Thomas Kluyver did a lot of the heavy lifting IIRC). Nothing really changed since then in this equation.
> 
> > It would satisfy my concerns if the changes just reversed the order (from beginner to advanced) of the `uv`/`pixi` option with the `pip` / `conda` option.
> 
> If we don't want to recommend `uv`/`pixi` for beginners because a number of people here think that that is too hard and therefore not the right choice, then I want to stay with Anaconda as the recommendation.

OK - but just to clarify - what, specifically, does Anaconda get you beyond the effect of:

```bash
pip3 install scipy matplotlib pandas statsmodels scikit-learn seaborn ipython jupyter
```

?  I think I'm saying that that issue is not as resolved as you think it is, and bears further discussion.

> 
> Off the top of my head:
> 
>     * many other popular packages that beginning to intermediate users may want/need, with integration testing between them,
> 
>     * a GUI for launching Python or Jupyter notebook/lab, and for navigating packages, with OS menu entries and other such niceties for beginners, and
> 
>     * not needing to use `pip` or `conda`, and hence not teaching the bad habits of installing into a base env or with `--user`.

`jupyter lab` from the command line is an adequate replacement for the GUI features of Anaconda, and the packages that I listed before were certainly enough to teach all three years of our data science courses at my last university.   Of course, if one has to go further, one is no longer working with beginners.

> > I think I'm saying that that issue is not as resolved as you think it is, and bears further discussion.
> 
> It is resolved for SciPy, because you have nothing new to say and I am unwilling to rehash the old argument - I have more productive and less draining things to do. You have a deep, and in my opinion unjustified, dislike of anything that smells like it comes from a company, or perhaps Anaconda in particular (maybe it now extends to Astral and Prefix too, dunno). That's your good right, and you get to use and teach with the tools of your preference. I'm not really interested in that bias though, nor in re-discussing this _exact same issue_.

Ah - no - I think you're assuming that my opinions are based on anti-corporate bias, but that's not the case.  In fact, the company angle hadn't even occurred to me for `uv`.   So I wonder whether you'd reconsider dismissing my opinions on these matters - they really are based on a lot of experience working with beginners - over a long period - and with many iterations.   I suspect I may be unusual in this conversation in working with many beginners, but I'm happy to be corrected.

The situation has absolutely changed from the very old discussions we've had on this in the past - as you can see from looking at the XKCD cartoon you referred to:

https://xkcd.com/1987

And in fact, the cartoon is, if anything, complaining about the great excess of different methods of install, so adding `uv` and `pixi` would only add more loops and lines.

And although it may be tiring, it is an important discussion, because the wrong install instructions will have a serious impact on Scipy's popularity.

It seems to me that the way forward here must be to move from assertion to argument, and if possible, to evidence.   I've put some thoughts below ("open questions").

Unfortunately, it's going to be very hard to get there if we are under threat of imminent veto - @rgommers - I guess you agree?   If so, I wonder whether we should move this important discussion to another place where there is more space for fruitful disagreement and debate?

## Open questions

* Is it reasonable to require beginners to open a terminal to run commands such as `pip install` or `jupyter lab`?  I think yes, and have experience to back that up - see for example https://textbook.nipraxis.org/installation_on_mac.html - but I'm happy for counterarguments.
* Is it reasonable to require beginners to edit their terminal configuration files?  I'd say no - in my experience this rapidly turns into a nightmare, especially on Windows.  I can firm that up with specifics if that's controversial.
* How often - in practice - do beginners find themselves with broken installations from some package incompatibility?   How hard - in practice - is it for beginners to get themselves out of this situation?   Can we make the escape route reasonably obvious for these situations? We should probably track down or recall actual examples for this - from StackOverflow or personal experience.
* How much does it matter that we give beginners commands that they don't understand - "magic"?   I'd say it does matter, and it matters more at the start than the middle of a course - the best students will always ask "why", and discomfort and anxiety usually follow.  I can give examples.
* How much do the current Anaconda terms of service matter?  Should we continue to worry that they will change again?
* If we do decide we need a one-click installer, how hard would it be to build our own, backed up by suitable CI, and that can be configured to install particular sets of packages?   I did this a long time ago for Mac : https://fullmonty.github.io - I guess it wouldn't be too hard to do that for Windows.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy.org/issues/comments/2558077097/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

These downloadable files require +little configuration, work on almost all setups, and provide all the +most commonly used scientific Python tools. +[Anaconda](https://www.anaconda.com/download/) works on Windows, Mac, +and Linux, and is best suited to beginning users. +Other options include: + +- [WinPython](https://winpython.github.io): Another free distribution + including scientific packages and the Spyder IDE; Windows only, but + more actively maintained and supports the latest Python 3 versions. +- [Pyzo](https://pyzo.org): A free distribution based on Anaconda + and the IEP interactive development environment; Supports Linux, + Windows, and Mac. + +{{< admonition note >}} +Users in large, non-university institutions may want to read Anaconda's +helpful blog on ["when is works on Windows, Mac, +and Linux, and is best suited to beginning users. +Other options include: + +- [WinPython](https://winpython.github.io): Another free distribution + including scientific Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-06-20T13:01:43Z","updated_at":"2024-10-03T15:09:49Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

This is really just to flag it up in case y'all would be interested to investigate - in case it's a Numpy 2.0 problem.  When porting Nipy to Numpy 2.0, I find we are getting multiple segfaults from extensions using compiled C code using the Numpy API.    See the reproducer below.   Compiling and running with Numpy 1.26.4 gives a clean test run, as has been the case up until now.   Is it possible we've run over a reference counting bug in Numpy?  Or that, for some reason, Numpy 2.0 has exposed our own reference counting bug, not previously triggered with Numpy < 2?

In the reproducer, the tests _nearly_ always trigger a segfault, with example traceback (from Apple interface) below - but it is not deterministic.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
virtualenv nipy-env
. ./nipy-env/bin/activate
git clone https://github.com/matthew-brett/nipy
cd nipy
git checkout numpy-20-fixes
pip install meson-python ninja cython numpy pytest spin
spin install
pytest nipy/labs/bindings/tests/test_numpy.py
```

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2024-06-20T13:01:43Z","updated_at":"2024-10-03T16:35:05Z","closed_at":"2024-10-03T16:35:04Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

This is really just to flag it up in case y'all would be interested to investigate - in case it's a Numpy 2.0 problem.  When porting Nipy to Numpy 2.0, I find we are getting multiple segfaults from extensions using compiled C code using the Numpy API.    See the reproducer below.   Compiling and running with Numpy 1.26.4 gives a clean test run, as has been the case up until now.   Is it possible we've run over a reference counting bug in Numpy?  Or that, for some reason, Numpy 2.0 has exposed our own reference counting bug, not previously triggered with Numpy < 2?

In the reproducer, the tests _nearly_ always trigger a segfault, with example traceback (from Apple interface) below - but it is not deterministic.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
virtualenv nipy-env
. ./nipy-env/bin/activate
git clone https://github.com/matthew-brett/nipy
cd nipy
git checkout numpy-20-fixes
pip install meson-python ninja cython numpy pytest spin
spin install
pytest nipy/labs/bindings/tests/test_numpy.py
```

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-10-03T17:52:28Z","updated_at":"2024-10-03T17:52:28Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

The docstring of `np.random.randint` implies that the `high` parameter should be an `int`, but it appears, in fact, it requires an `np.intc`:

```python
[ins] In [2]: MAX_INT = np.iinfo(int).max

[ins] In [3]: MAX_INT
Out[3]: 9223372036854775807

[ins] In [4]: np.random.randint(MAX_INT)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ValueError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[4], line 1
----> 1 np.random.randint(MAX_INT)

File numpy\\random\\mtrand.pyx:796, in numpy.random.mtrand.RandomState.randint()

File numpy\\random\\_bounded_integers.pyx:2881, in numpy.random._bounded_integers._rand_int32()

ValueError: high is out of bounds for int32
```

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
MAX_INT = np.iinfo(int).max
np.random.randint(MAX_INT)
```


### Error message:

```shell
ValueError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[4], line 1
----> 1 np.random.randint(MAX_INT)

File numpy\\random\\mtrand.pyx:796, in numpy.random.mtrand.RandomState.randint()

File numpy\\random\\_bounded_integers.pyx:2881, in numpy.random._bounded_integers._rand_int32()

ValueError: high is out of bounds for int32
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

2.1.1

{"action":"closed","number":563,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nipy/pulls/563","id":1829987216,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAAnNKM5tE2OQ","html_url":"https://github.com/nipy/nipy/pull/563","diff_url":"https://github.com/nipy/nipy/pull/563.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/nipy/nipy/pull/563.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nipy/issues/563","number":563,"state":"closed","locked":false,"title":"DOC: Fix activation map tutorial","user":{"login":"ntolley","id":55253912,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU1MjUzOTEy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/55253912?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley","html_url":"https://github.com/ntolley","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Currently the documentation for [how to plot activation maps](https://nipy.org/nipy/labs/viz.html) throws an error when run on a clean environment:
```
TypeError: slice indices must be integers or None or have an __index__ method
```

The issue is these lines
```py
x_map, y_map, z_map = coord_transform(x, y, z, mni_sform_inv)
map = np.zeros((182, 218, 182))
map[x_map-30:x_map+30, y_map-3:y_map+3, z_map-10:z_map+10] = 1
```
`x_map` is returned as a 0 dimensional `np.array` which can't be used for indexing

The current fix is a bit of a band-aid. Would it be better to modify `coord_transform()` so that it actually returns integers?","created_at":"2024-04-18T17:01:15Z","updated_at":"2024-10-05T23:35:22Z","closed_at":"2024-10-05T23:35:22Z","merged_at":"2024-10-05T23:35:22Z","merge_commit_sha":"9f13f5ed5c48196ca55bd36f3e86624178818e8a","assignee":null,"assignees":[],"requested_reviewers":[],"requested_teams":[],"labels":[],"milestone":null,"draft":false,"commits_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nipy/pulls/563/commits","review_comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nipy/pulls/563/comments","review_comment_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nipy/pulls/comments{/number}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nipy/issues/563/comments","statuses_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nipy/statuses/9f13f5ed5c48196ca55bd36f3e86624178818e8a","head":{"label":"ntolley:patch-1","ref":"patch-1","sha":"9f13f5ed5c48196ca55bd36f3e86624178818e8a","user":{"login":"ntolley","id":55253912,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU1MjUzOTEy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/55253912?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley","html_url":"https://github.com/ntolley","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ntolley/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"repo":{"id":788554804,"node_id":"R_kgDOLwBkNA","name":"nipy","full_name":"ntolley/nipy","private":false}},"base":{"label":"nipy:main","ref":"main","sha":"181ea05968fd36576ace16b78210ec8aa8dc2307","user":{"login":"nipy","id":233707,"node_id optimize.minimize: Expose LINCOA, BOBYQA, NEWUOA, and UOBYQA","user":{"login":"nbelakovski","id":864630,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg2NDYzMA==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/864630?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/nbelakovski","html_url":"https://github.com/nbelakovski","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nbelakovski/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nbelakovski/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nbelakovski/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nbelakovski/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nbelakovski/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nbelakovski/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nbelakovski/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nbelakovski/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/nbelakovski/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":37340735,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MDczNQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/enhancement","name":"enhancement","color":"bfe5bf","default":true,"description":"A new feature or improvement"},{"id":37341011,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MTAxMQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/scipy.optimize","name":"scipy.optimize","color":"0366d6","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":12,"created_at":"2024-09-12T20:51:52Z","updated_at":"2024-09-14T10:09:01Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

With the pending closure of https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20964, PRIMA will be integrated with SciPy, and while the primary goal of that PR and the issue it closes is to update COBYLA, PRIMA contains 4 other algorithms for derivative free optimization. They are:

* LINCOA - LINearly Constrained Optimization algorithm - for problems with linear constraints and bound constraints (bot no nonlinear constraints)
* BOBYQA - Bound Optimization BY Quadratic Approximation - for problems with bound constraints, but no linear/nonlinear constraints
* UOBYQA - Unconstrained Optimization BY Quadratic Approximation - for unconstrained problems
* NEWUOA - Newer algorithm for unconstrained problems.

More information can be found at @zaikunzhang's website: https://www.zhangzk.net/software.html

Since PRIMA will already be integrated in SciPy with the above PR, exposing these should be rather simple. I could do these myself, and I would propose to add them one by one to smooth out the review process, but these could also be a nice set of "good first issue" for folks interested in contributing to SciPy. I wonder if the maintainers have someone in mind who wants to contribute and can add these, or perhaps we can mark this issue as a good first issue and leave it up for a month or so to see if we have any bites?

Like I said I'd be happy to get this done myself and I'd even be willing to take responsibility for maintaining it in the future, but I thought it might be prudent to pause and ask if there are others who would like to take this opportunity to contribute.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

_No response_

Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

With the pending closure of https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20964, PRIMA will be integrated with SciPy, and while the primary goal of that PR and the issue it closes is to update COBYLA, PRIMA contains 4 other algorithms for derivative free optimization. They are:

* LINCOA - LINearly Constrained Optimization algorithm - for problems with linear constraints and bound constraints (bot no nonlinear constraints)
* BOBYQA - Bound Optimization BY Quadratic Approximation - for problems with bound constraints, but no linear/nonlinear constraints
* UOBYQA - Unconstrained Optimization BY Quadratic Approximation - for unconstrained problems
* NEWUOA - Newer algorithm for unconstrained problems.

More information can be found at @zaikunzhang's website: https://www.zhangzk.net/software.html

Since PRIMA will already be integrated in SciPy with the above PR, exposing these should be rather simple. I could do these myself, and I would propose to add them one by one to smooth out the review process, but these could also be a nice set of "good first issue" for folks interested in contributing to SciPy. I wonder if the maintainers have someone in mind who wants to contribute and can add these, or perhaps we can mark this issue as a good first issue and leave it up for a month or so to see if we have any bites?

Like I said I'd be happy to get this done myself and I'd even be willing to take responsibility for maintaining it in the future, but I thought it might be prudent to pause and ask if there are others who would like to take this opportunity to contribute.

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21256/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21256/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2250205925","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21256#issuecomment-2250205925","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21256","id":2250205925,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6GH2rl","user":{"login":"matthew-brett","id":67612,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY3NjEy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/67612?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett","html_url":"https://github.com/matthew-brett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-25T12:28:59Z","updated_at":"2024-07-25T12:28:59Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Just to unpack - there are two problems here - you've pointed to them both, but just to explain for our readers...

The first, at https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/main/scipy/io/matlab/_mio4.py#L359 - is that we are (I was) multiplying a dtype.itemsize (that is always of type int32) by the number of elements (a Python int). This would not overflow in Numpy < 2, because the result gets cast to `int64`:

```python
[nav] In [5]: type(np.int32(1) * 1)
Out[5]: numpy.int64
```

but it does in Numpy 2, because the result is now `int32`:

```python
[ins] In [2]: type(np.int32(1) * 1)
Out[2]: numpy.int32
```

and, indeed, I think the right fix is to cast as int or maybe `np.intp` (to preserve the overflow check) before multiplying.

The second is at https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/main/scipy/io/matlab/_mio4.py#L177

Here the elements in `dims` are constrained by the format to be `int32`, but the product of the dims and the itemsize (the size of the array) need not be within int32.  The calculation in Numpy < 2 and Numpy 2 gives an int32 result, so this was a bug before and after Numpy 2.

I think the right thing to do there is a slight refactor to use the same fast-product trick as in the code above, and replace:

```python
        num_bytes = dt.itemsize
        for d in dims:
            num_bytes *= d
```

with something like:

```python
        num_bytes = reduce(lambda x, y: x*y, dims, np.intp(dt.itemsize))  # fast product.
```

The trick will be making a good test!

Describe your issue.

loadmat function failure , 
RuntimeWarning: overflow encountered in long_scalars
  remaining_bytes = hdr.dtype.itemsize * n  

this is because  hdr.dtype.itemsize  type is numpy.int32, will convert the address postion from python int type into numpy 32bit int type , will for large file , 64 bit address is enough , but 32nit address will be overflowed 

suggest:
![suggest1](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/25c657b7-0bc8-4f9d-911d-8e94112edc1b)
![suggest2](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/22027651-4ba3-4d60-ba76-4bd242ff5441)



### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy import io
data=io.loadmat('data.mat') # data.at file size 2.45GB , with over 800,000 parameters
```


### Error message

```shell
:\Programs\Python\Python38\lib\site-packages\scipy\io\matlab\_mio4.py:358: RuntimeWarning: overflow encountered in long_scalars
  remaining_bytes = hdr.dtype.itemsize * n
W:\Programs\Python\Python38\lib\site-packages\scipy\io\matlab\_mio4.py:176: RuntimeWarning: overflow encountered in long_scalars
  num_bytes *= d
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "w:\Users\jwajus3\Downloads\Case2_Tier1_8HP_HP_overloadCooling52_L_120_20211213\test.py", line 2, in <module>
    data=io.loadmat('data.mat')
  File "W:\Programs\Python\Python38\lib\site-packages\scipy\io\matlab\_mio.py", line 226, in loadmat
    matfile_dict = MR.get_variables(variable_names)
  File "W:\Programs\Python\Python38\lib\site-packages\scipy\io\matlab\_mio4.py", line 405, in get_variables
    mdict[name] = self.read_var_array(hdr)
  File "W:\Programs\Python\Python38\lib\site-packages\scipy\io\matlab\_mio4.py", line 380, in read_var_array
    return self._matrix_reader.array_from_header(header, process)
  File "W:\Programs\Python\Python38\lib\site-packages\scipy\io\matlab\_mio4.py", line 141, in array_from_header
    arr = self.read_full_array(hdr)
  File "W:\Programs\Python\Python38\lib\site-packages\scipy\io\matlab\_mio4.py", line 211, in read_full_array

Installation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":"Problems installing or compiling NumPy"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-07-30T08:28:05Z","updated_at":"2024-07-30T13:28:18Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Steps to reproduce:

I checked the license of NumPy via "pip show numpy". I found NumPy uses LGPL-licensed library **libquadmath** . 
It says that the file location is "numpy.libs\libopenb*.dll"
However, in my environment, there is no libopenb*.dll but the files in the folder (numpy.libs) is as following:

> .load-order-numpy-2.0.0
> libscipy_openblas64_-fb1711452d4d8cee9f276fd1449ee5c7.dll
> msvcp140-8021418012832a07a8ca5105a33b1086.dll

Where is libopenb*.dll? Or is it mistake for libscipy_openblas64*.dll?
I wanted to know because I plan to make an exe by PyInstaller and need to know which libraries should be outside the exe to be replaceable by users, which meets LGPL license. 
(For other libraries such as "GCC runtime library",  numpy.libs\libgfortran*.dll was also not found though I didn't check all)


Installation: pip install numpy
NumPy version: 2.0.0
OS: Windows 10
Python: 3.9.0
Using miniconda environment. 
(Though I know it is not recommended to mix conda and pip, I did because I need a conda-forge package for some reasons.)

command:
pip show numpy

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-06-17T20:36:48Z","updated_at":"2024-06-17T20:36:49Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

Over at https://github.com/nipy/nipy - out Meson-based build works without error on Numpy 1.26.4.   But, in the same virtual environment, with the single change of installing Numpy 2.0 - we get multiple build errors of form:

```
nipy/algorithms/graph/_graph.cpython-311-darwin.so.p/_graph.c:3042:13: error: call to undeclared function 'PyDataType_ELSIZE'; ISO C99 and later do not support implicit function declarations [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
  __pyx_r = PyDataType_ELSIZE(__pyx_v_self);
```

with many Numpy-defines  missing - `PyDataType_SUBARRAY`, `PyDataType_FIELDS`, `PyArray_MultiIter_NUMITER` and so on.   These are all in a generated Cython file, using Cython 3.0.10.

Is it possible I missed some upgrade to Cython or similar that includes such defines for Numpy 2.0?

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
git clone https://github.com/nipy/nipy
cd nipy
pip install cython


followed by:

```bash
pip install .
```

or:

```bash
meson build
cd build
ninja -j 1
```
```

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-06-17T20:36:48Z","updated_at":"2024-06-17T22:06:40Z","closed_at":"2024-06-17T22:06:40Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

Over at https://github.com/nipy/nipy - out Meson-based build works without error on Numpy 1.26.4.   But, in the same virtual environment, with the single change of installing Numpy 2.0 - we get multiple build errors of form:

```
nipy/algorithms/graph/_graph.cpython-311-darwin.so.p/_graph.c:3042:13: error: call to undeclared function 'PyDataType_ELSIZE'; ISO C99 and later do not support implicit function declarations [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
  __pyx_r = PyDataType_ELSIZE(__pyx_v_self);
```

with many Numpy-defines  missing - `PyDataType_SUBARRAY`, `PyDataType_FIELDS`, `PyArray_MultiIter_NUMITER` and so on.   These are all in a generated Cython file, using Cython 3.0.10.

Is it possible I missed some upgrade to Cython or similar that includes such defines for Numpy 2.0?

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
git clone https://github.com/nipy/nipy
cd nipy
pip install cython
```

followed by:

```bash
pip install .
```

or:

```bash
meson build
cd build
ninja -j 1
```

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-06-20T13:01:43Z","updated_at":"2024-06-21T13:43:18Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

This is really just to flag it up in case y'all would be interested to investigate - in case it's a Numpy 2.0 problem.  When porting Nipy to Numpy 2.0, I find we are getting multiple segfaults from extensions using compiled C code using the Numpy API.    See the reproducer below.   Compiling and running with Numpy 1.26.4 gives a clean test run, as has been the case up until now.   Is it possible we've run over a reference counting bug in Numpy?  Or that, for some reason, Numpy 2.0 has exposed our own reference counting bug, not previously triggered with Numpy < 2?

In the reproducer, the tests _nearly_ always trigger a segfault, with example traceback (from Apple interface) below - but it is not deterministic.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
virtualenv nipy-env
. ./nipy-env/bin/activate
git clone https://github.com/matthew-brett/nipy
cd nipy
git checkout numpy-20-fixes
pip install meson-python ninja cython numpy pytest spin
spin install
pytest nipy/labs/bindings/tests/test_numpy.py
```

Question","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":"Question about NumPy usage or development"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2024-03-27T14:51:54Z","updated_at":"2024-04-02T09:39:58Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

Currently, `np.abs` for the most negative integer of a given types cannot return the absolute value in the same type, so instead wraps and gives a negative value. This is surprising, and could be avoided by promoting to the unsigned type (e.g.,  `i1`->`u1`). However, this may have its own issues if further calculations are done, so really not sure what is the best option. (Hence, not sure whether this is a bug or a question...)

Note that passing in `dtype='u1'` works, though one then has to also give `casting='unsafe'` - even though in this case it is of course not unsafe at all...



### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
np.abs(np.array([-128, -127], 'i1'))
# array([-128,  127], dtype=int8)
np.abs(np.array([-128, -127], 'i1'), dtype='u1', casting='unsafe')
# array([128, 129], dtype=uint8)
```


### Error message:

_No response_

Pandas version checks

- [X] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [X] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [X] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd
pd.to_datetime(['31 May 2023', '1 Apr 2023'])
```


### Issue Description

When the first date entries have "May" as the month, the parser assumes the months are written out as "month as locale’s full name" (format code `%B`), and then, when it hits an abbreviated month (correct format code `%b` - abbreviated name), it gives an error:

```
ValueError: time data "1 Apr 2023" doesn't match format "%d %B %Y", at position 1. You might want to try:
    - passing `format` if your strings have a consistent format;
    - passing `format='ISO8601'` if your strings are all ISO8601 but not necessarily in exactly the same format;
    - passing `format='mixed'`, and the format will be inferred for each element individually. You might want to use `dayfirst` alongside this.
```

### Expected Behavior

It would be very good if the parser remained agnostic as to whether the month specifier was abbreviated until after it had seen a month other than "May".

I mean, I think it is reasonable to consider a risky guess as a bug. Here, when the first date has "May" as the month, the chances of full-month-format (`%B`) being correct are - difficult to estimate - it would depend on how frequent full-month and abbreviated-month formats are in the wild.  My guess is that abbreviated month formats are more common, so the current guess is more often wrong than right. Pandas version checks

- [X] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported.

- [X] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas.

- [X] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas.


### Reproducible Example

```python
import pandas as pd
pd.to_datetime(['31 May 2023', '1 Apr 2023'])
```


### Issue Description

When the first date entries have "May" as the month, the parser assumes the months are written out as "month as locale’s full name" (format code `%B`), and then, when it hits an abbreviated month (correct format code `%b` - abbreviated name), it gives an error:

```
ValueError: time data "1 Apr 2023" doesn't match format "%d %B %Y", at position 1. You might want to try:
    - passing `format` if your strings have a consistent format;
    - passing `format='ISO8601'` if your strings are all ISO8601 but not necessarily in exactly the same format;
    - passing `format='mixed'`, and the format will be inferred for each element individually. You might want to use `dayfirst` alongside this.
```

### Expected Behavior

It would be very good if the parser remained agnostic as to whether the month specifier was abbreviated until after it had seen a month other than "May".

import pandas as pd
pd.to_datetime(['31 May 2023', '1 Apr 2023'])
```


### Issue Description

When the first date entries have "May" as the month, the parser assumes the months are written out as "month as locale’s full name" (format code `%B`), and then, when it hits an abbreviated month (correct format code `%b` - abbreviated name), it gives an error:

```
ValueError: time data "1 Apr 2023" doesn't match format "%d %B %Y", at position 1. You might want to try:
    - passing `format` if your strings have a consistent format;
    - passing `format='ISO8601'` if your strings are all ISO8601 but not necessarily in exactly the same format;
    - passing `format='mixed'`, and the format will be inferred for each element individually. You might want to use `dayfirst` alongside this.
```

### Expected Behavior

It would be very good if the parser remained agnostic as to whether the month specifier was abbreviated until after it had seen a month other than "May".

(over at https://github.com/nipy/nipy) have a customized doctest `OutputChecker` class for a few new directives - https://github.com/matthew-brett/nipy/blob/pytest/nipy/conftest.py#L152

I have tried to apply this output checker by monkey-patching `doctest` in `conftest.py`: 

```python
doctest.OutputChecker = NipyOutputChecker
```

I can see, with a debug test in a `.py` file, that shows me that, when I get to the doctest, `doctest.OutputChecker` is my checker.  

https://github.com/matthew-brett/nipy/blob/pytest/nipy/algorithms/statistics/formula/formulae.py#L95

But the checkers `check_output` never gets called.

I wondered idly whether this is because y'all enforce your `OutputChecker` in `DoctestModulePlus`:

https://github.com/scientific-python/pytest-doctestplus/blob/main/pytest_doctestplus/plugin.py#L268

What is the best way to plumb in my OutputChecker, when using Adding axis to the right is perplexing, specially when `atleast_2d`  adds to the left and numpy broadcasting behavior always grows to the left.

Is there a good reason for this? I see there's a PR to customize behavior via kwarg but the default behavior is kept.

I suspect this is more of a bug/ historical accident than a feature but happy to be corrected. This is relevant for libraries that try to emulate numpy API but understandably don't want to frustrate users with obscure behavior.

Describe your issue.

I have identified a bug when using sklearn.decomposition.PCA which is reported here:
https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/discussions/27946

I feel that it is appropriate to report the issue here also since the `svd_solver='full'` argument calls scipy.linalg.svd to perform the SVD. The sklearn discussion has gone quiet and I feel that it is necessary to update the documentation to reflect this issue. Essentially, dtype float32 input arrays produce different (worse) results than the exact same array with dtype float64 i.e. the maximum discrepancy (`np.max(np.abs(X_diff))`) changes from one dtype to the next. I have synthetic datasets that can be used to test this  (`X_t_test_noise_float32.npy` and `X_t_test_noise_float64.npy`), but I don't know how to supply them.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA

X = np.load('X_t_test_noise_float32.npy')
# X = np.load('X_t_test_noise_float64.npy')

# Percentage of variance to retain in PCA
variance = 0.999

# PCA model fitting and transformations
pca_model = PCA(n_components=variance, svd_solver='auto')
X_compressed = pca_model.fit_transform(X)
X_reconstructed = pca_model.inverse_transform(X_compressed)
print('Original shape:', X.shape)
print('Compressed shape:', X_compressed.shape)
print('Compression factor:', X.shape[1]//X_compressed.shape[1])

print('X_reconstructed mean:', np.mean(X_reconstructed))
print('X_reconstructed max:', np.max(X_reconstructed))
print('X_reconstructed min:', np.min(X_reconstructed))

# Calculate root-mean-square error with respect to the original data
X_diff = X - X_reconstructed
rmse = np.sqrt(np.mean(X_diff**2))
print('RMSE:', rmse)
print('Max discrepancy:', np.max(np.abs(X_diff)))
```

```

After installing `numpy 2.0.0.dev ` via the Python wheel on GitHub's macOS-14 image ([running on M1 hardware](https://github.blog/changelog/2024-01-30-github-actions-introducing-the-new-m1-macos-runner-available-to-open-source/)), importing `numpy.fft` fails with an error about a missing `libc++` library.

I am afraid I am not able to help much with debugging beyond this report, since I don't have access to the architecture myself (i.e. outside of GitHub Actions).

### Reproduce the code example:

After installing `numyp` via pip (on Python 3.12):
```
python -m pip install --pre \
  -i https://pypi.anaconda.org/scientific-python-nightly-wheels/simple \
  numpy
```
The following fails:
```
python -c 'import numpy.fft'
```

Here's a repo with a GitHub workflow reproducing the issue:
https://github.com/mstimberg/numpy_m1_test

Describe your issue.

I have identified a bug when using sklearn.decomposition.PCA which is reported here:
https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/discussions/27946

I feel that it is appropriate to report the issue here also since the `svd_solver='full'` argument calls scipy.linalg.svd to perform the SVD. The sklearn discussion has gone quiet and I feel that it is necessary to update the documentation to reflect this issue. Essentially, dtype float32 input arrays produce different (worse) results than the exact same array with dtype float64 i.e. the maximum discrepancy (`np.max(np.abs(X_diff))`) changes from one dtype to the next. I have synthetic datasets that can be used to test this  (`X_t_test_noise_float32.npy` and `X_t_test_noise_float64.npy`), but I don't know how to supply them.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA

X = np.load('X_t_test_noise_float32.npy')
# X = np.load('X_t_test_noise_float64.npy')

# Percentage of variance to retain in PCA
variance = 0.999

# PCA model fitting and transformations
pca_model = PCA(n_components=variance, svd_solver='auto')
X_compressed = pca_model.fit_transform(X)
X_reconstructed = pca_model.inverse_transform(X_compressed)
print('Original shape:', X.shape)
print('Compressed shape:', X_compressed.shape)
print('Compression factor:', X.shape[1]//X_compressed.shape[1])

print('X_reconstructed mean:', np.mean(X_reconstructed))
print('X_reconstructed max:', np.max(X_reconstructed))
print('X_reconstructed min:', np.min(X_reconstructed))

# Calculate root-mean-square error with respect to the original data
X_diff = X - X_reconstructed
rmse = np.sqrt(np.mean(X_diff**2))
print('RMSE:', rmse)
print('Max discrepancy:', np.max(np.abs(X_diff)))
```

```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nibabel/issues/1287","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nibabel","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nibabel/issues/1287/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nibabel/issues/1287/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nibabel/issues/1287/events","html_url":"https://github.com/nipy/nibabel/issues/1287","id":2044361815,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAwTOM552nxX","number":1287,"title":"Request: stop throwing ExpiredDeprecationError","user":{"login":"coalsont","id":1513170,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1MTMxNzA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1513170?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/coalsont","html_url":"https://github.com/coalsont","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/coalsont/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/coalsont/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/coalsont/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/coalsont/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/coalsont/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/coalsont/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/coalsont/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/coalsont/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/coalsont/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2023-12-15T20:59:05Z","updated_at":"2024-01-16T23:50:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Nibabel is used in research settings, which somewhat often results in a tool getting written and then abandoned by its original author (while still being useful to users).  Fast forward a few years, the original nibabel API deprecates something, another few years, and on a new nibabel version release, suddenly the abandoned code errors in new installations, not because it is doing anything different or the input files changed, but merely because nobody was maintaining the code to edit it for the API enhancement, and people using the tool have to either hold back the nibabel version, or find a developer to edit and test the API change.

As long as you don't have a need to reuse the same function name for a different purpose (and it is almost always easy to find an alternative name instead), I think there is very little to be gained from making calls to it throw an error (as opposed to a warning), while throwing causes pain for end users.  "Streamlining" by removing code just because a newer/improved alternative is available doesn't seem particularly compelling to me (it is an extra step to delete the code, and for what real benefit?) - I would think you could have the documentation move deprecated functions to a less prominent location, and probably make most autocompleters not display them at the top of the suggestions, without outright deleting them.  What percent install size does it actually save?  What other benefits do you see that could justify the pain to users when an old tool stops working?

Relevant search shows 50 issues on github:

https://github.com/search?q=ExpiredDeprecationError&type=issues

the main point though, is that we believe that the user has used the function - here `get_data` - without understanding the ambiguities of its return value - and they will therefore make themselves prone to unexpected errors when using their code on other data.   Of course that might be wrong - they may have understood that - but there's no way of knowing - and it's more likely that they did not understand.   They will likely ignore any warnings - most of us do.   So the error is the only way to signal they need to go back and review their code to see what they did intend. Right - this is judgement call, with some knobs to tune.   My own threshold is "the code knows that something awful is very likely to happen".  You might not accept our argument on that.

I'm sure you know - but all Python libraries I know do this, deprecate and then remove - hence - for example, we have to keep updating to keep up to date with expiring deprecations on Numpy and Scipy features.

Of course Nibabel is somewhere between a library and an application - another knob to tune.

Fast forward a few years, the original nibabel API deprecates something, another few years, and on a new nibabel version release, suddenly the abandoned code errors in new installations, not because it is doing anything different or the input files changed, but merely because nobody was maintaining the code to edit it for the API enhancement, and people using the tool have to either hold back the nibabel version, or find a developer to edit and test the API change.

As long as you don't have a need to reuse the same function name for a different purpose (and it is almost always easy to find an alternative name instead), I think there is very little to be gained from making calls to it throw an error (as opposed to a warning), while throwing causes pain for end users.  "Streamlining" by removing code just because a newer/improved alternative is available doesn't seem particularly compelling to me (it is an extra step to delete the code, and for what real benefit?) - I would think you could have the documentation move deprecated functions to a less prominent location, and probably make most autocompleters not display them at the top of the suggestions, without outright deleting them.  What percent install size does it actually save?  What other benefits do you see that could justify the pain to users when an old tool stops working?

Relevant search shows 50 issues on github:

https://github.com/search?q=ExpiredDeprecationError&type=issues

Alternative title: "ExpiredDeprecationError considered harmful".","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nibabel/issues/1287/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nibabel/issues/1287/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nibabel/issues/comments/1900094069","html_url":"https://github.com/nipy/nibabel/issues/1287#issuecomment-1900094069","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nibabel/issues/1287","id":1900094069,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAwTOM5xQSJ1","user":{"login":"matthew-brett","id":67612,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY3NjEy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/67612?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett","html_url":"https://github.com/matthew-brett","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthew-brett/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-01-19T09:56:18Z","updated_at":"2024-01-19T09:56:18Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Just to be a bit clearer about what I mean.  Here is the Numpy way of doing deprecations and errors:

Numpy 1.21.6

```
[ins] In [2]: np.array(1, dtype=np.float)
<ipython-input-2-11e776fef755>:1: DeprecationWarning: `np.float` is a deprecated alias for the builtin `float`. To silence this warning, use `float` by itself. Doing this will not modify any behavior and is safe. If you specifically wanted the numpy scalar type, use `np.float64` here.
Deprecated in NumPy 1.20; for more details and guidance: https://numpy.org/devdocs/release/1.20.0-notes.html#deprecations
  np.array(1, dtype=np.float)
Out[2]: array(1.)
```

Numpy 1.26.3:

```
[ins] In [2]: np.array(1, dtype=np.float)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[2], line 1
----> 1 np.array(1, dtype=np.float)

File /Volumes/zorg/mb312/.virtualenvs/test/lib/python3.10/site-packages/numpy/__init__.py:324, in __getattr__(attr)
    319     warnings.warn(
    320         f"In the future `np.{attr}` will be defined as the "
    321         "corresponding NumPy scalar.", FutureWarning, stacklevel=2)
    323 if attr in __former_attrs__:
--> 324     raise AttributeError(__former_attrs__[attr])
    326 if attr == 'testing':
    327     import numpy.testing as testing

AttributeError: module 'numpy' has no attribute 'float'.
`np.float` was a deprecated alias for the builtin `float`. To avoid this error in existing code, use `float` by itself. Doing this will not modify any behavior and is safe. If you specifically wanted the numpy scalar type, use `np.float64` here.
The aliases was originally deprecated in NumPy 1.20; for more details and guidance see the original release note at:
    https://numpy.org/devdocs/release/1.20.0-notes.html#deprecations
```

So, not an explicit `ExpiredDeprecationWarning`, just another slightly less descriptive error name for the same thing.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nibabel/issues/comments/1900094069/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

When I try to compute the weighted mean of an array that includes `inf`, and that element is given a weight of 0, I'm getting a RuntimeWarning, and a nan result.
This isn't the case when the weight is just slightly larger than zero.

Drilling this down, I reached np.multiply(), but I'm not familiar enough with python/C to dig deeper..

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import sys

import numpy as np

print(np.average([1, 1], weights=[1, 0]))
# 1.0
print(np.average([np.inf, 1], weights=[1, 1]))
# inf
print(np.average([np.inf, 1], weights=[1, 0]))
# inf
print(np.average([np.inf, 1], weights=[0, 1]))
# nan
# RuntimeWarning: invalid value encountered in multiply avg = np.multiply(a, wgt,

eps = sys.float_info.epsilon
print(np.average([np.inf, 1], weights=[0+eps, 1]))
# inf
```


### Error message:

```shell
C:\Users\REDACTED\anaconda3\envs\REDACTED\lib\site-packages\numpy\lib\function_base.py:550: RuntimeWarning: invalid value encountered in multiply
  avg = np.multiply(a, wgt,
```

On Sun, 10 Dec 2023 at 17:34, Nathan Goldbaum ***@***.***>
wrote:

> I see in the SciPy issue that R’s behavior is prior art for handling zero
> weights with special behavior. R’s docs indicates that weighted averages
> have special handling for zero weight values - pretty neat. As far as I can
> tell R has always behaved that way.
>
> Making this change in numpy needs to clear a high bar because doing so is
> a behavior change. Since it will produce different results in a
> calculation, the behavior change must be justified given that this will
> inevitably break user code that is relying on this behavior somehow.
>
> Here is a code search that finds a number of usages in downstream code
> <https://github.com/search?q=np.average+weights&type=code>. One way to
> gauge the impact of such a change would be to patch numpy to have this
> behavior and then run e.g. the scikit-learn tests and see if there are
> failures. I wouldn’t be surprised if there are.
>
> It’s not just whether or not changing numpy’s behavior would make it
> easier for newcomers to learn numpy, we also have to gauge downstream
> impact and decide whether the code churn is worth the pain of dealing with
> an imperfection in numpy that’s existed for a long time.
>

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2023-08-30T14:12:31Z","updated_at":"2023-12-20T15:14:46Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

Please forgive this as-yet-unformed question - but I am struggling with the documentation for `quantile`.   The documentation has this:

"""
Given a vector V of length n, the q-th quantile of V is the value q of the way from the minimum to the maximum in a sorted copy of V.
"""

However - it's not clear to me what this means.   

Assume in what follows that V is sorted.

First "minimum" and "maximum" would normally refer to `min(V)` and `max(V)` but I'm sure we actually mean the minimum and maximum index - so 0 and `n - 1` in Python terms.   So I think this is referring to the distance along the line starting at 0 and ending at n-1, and therefore the "quantile positions" (I made up this term) corresponding to each value in V are:

```python
qps = np.arange(n) / (n - 1)
```

Is that correct?

Is it correct to use the term "plotting positions" for my "quantile positions" `qps`?

Next we have this:

"""
The values and distances of the two nearest neighbors as well as the method parameter will determine the quantile if the normalized ranking does not match the location of q exactly.
"""

I was interested in the cases where the normalized ranking *does* match the location of q exactly - where I'm assuming the "location of q" means my `qps` above.

So I'm taking q for each element `V[i]` to be `qps[i]` - is that right?  Therefore `q = np.quantile(V, qps[i], method=m) == V[i]` for all valid values of m?

If so, it's easy to show that, for many of the methods, that isn't the case.

Just as an example:

```python
n = 4
V = np.arange(n)  # The values.
qps = np.arange(n) / (n - 1)  # Quantile positions
# linear is the default
assert np.allclose(  # Passes
    np.quantile(V, qps, method='linear'), V)
assert np.allclose(. # Fails
    np.quantile(V, qps, method='closest_observation'), V)
```

Have I interpreted these correctly?  If so, or even if not, maybe the docstring needs a rewrite?

### Idea or request for content:

My worry it that the formula from [1] does provide a general framework for describing all implementations, but at the expense of making it quite a bit harder to follow the default implementation.

Just as a side-note - I find `x` to be a little confusing as the name for a sorted copy of `a` because we are also implying a plot with plotting positions on the x-axis (which are not `x`) and quantile values on the y-axis (which are not the same as, but closer to `x`). builds and tests clean on Python 3.8, so this just acknowledges and advertises that. We could also follow [SPEC0](https://scientific-python.org/specs/spec-0000/), which would have us drop Python <3.10 at this point. IMO Nipy doesn't need to be as conservative as nibabel here, so bumping to 3.10+ would be fine.

https://github.com/nipy/nipy/blob/ca70df937b6943d2fbc37b65c463bb78bab28f22/pyproject.toml#L62-L67

However, this is not clearly documented.

Worse, the ruff setting [target-version](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/settings/#target-version), the minimum Python version to target, is set to Python 3.9:
https://github.com/nipy/nipy/blob/ca70df937b6943d2fbc37b65c463bb78bab28f22/pyproject.toml#L77-L79

How about adding the [`requires-python`](https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/guides/writing-pyproject-toml/#python-requires) key in the [`[project]`](https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/guides/writing-pyproject-toml/#static-vs-dynamic-metadata) table? MRG: Test and advertise support for Python 3.8+

Nipy builds and tests clean on Python 3.8, so this just acknowledges and advertises that. We could also follow [SPEC0](https://scientific-python.org/specs/spec-0000/), which would have us drop Python <3.10 at this point. IMO Nipy doesn't need to be as conservative as nibabel here, so bumping to 3.10+ would be fine.

Fix picked up by `ruff` included.

Closes #557.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nipy/commits/fe424cdfdd15fd96a60c7945e91584997793f24f"}]}

MRG: Cython: declare pointers one per line

None of these declarations was incorrect, but Cython now warns about them. The developers are worried that people will write “cdef int* a, b” and think that “b” is an “int*” rather than an “int”. Reducing the number of warnings from Cython makes it easier to notice warnings that might represent real problems.

See also https://groups.google.com/g/cython-users/c/MA8I1wmoT0Q for discussion.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nipy/commits/e39c4efc3b8cb9a9424323240be30887d8d76856"}]}

+[project]
+name = "nipy"
+dynamic = ['version']
+license = {file = "LICENSE"}
+description  = 'A python package for analysis of neuroimaging data'
+readme = 'README.rst'
+classifiers = ["Development Status :: 3 - Alpha",
+               "Environment :: Console",
+               "Intended Audience :: Science/Research",
+               "License :: OSI Approved :: BSD License",
+               "Operating System :: OS Independent",
+               "Programming Language :: Python",
+               "Topic :: Scientific/Engineering"]
+dependencies = [
+    'numpy>=1.22',
+    'scipy>=1.8',
+    'nibabel>=3.2',
+    'sympy>=1.9',
+    'transforms3d'
+]
+
+[[project.maintainers]]
+name = 'nipy developers'
+email = 'neuroimaging@python.org'
+
+[project.urls]
+homepage = 'https://nipy.org/nipy'
+documentation = 'https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable'
+source = 'https://github.com/nipy/nipy'
+download = 'https://pypi.org/project/nipy/#files'
+tracker = 'https://github.com/nipy/nipy/issues'
+
+[project.optional-dependencies]
+build = [
+    'meson-python>=0.13',
+    'wheel',
+    'setuptools>=67',
+    'ninja',
+    'Cython>=3',
+    'numpy>=1.22',
+    'spin==0.3',
+    'build',
+]
+default = [
+    'numpy>=1.22',
+    'scipy>=1.8',
+    'nibabel>=3.2',
+    'sympy>=1.9',
+    'transforms3d'
+]
+developer = [
+    'pre-commit',
+    'rtoml',
+]
+docs = [
+    'sphinx>=7.0',
+    'numpydoc>=1.6.0',
+    'matplotlib',
+    'texext',
+    'ipython'
+]
+optional = [
+    'matplotlib>=3',
+]
+test = [
+    'matplotlib>=3',
+    'pytest>=7.2',
+    'pytest-cov>=4.0',
+    'pytest-doctestplus'
+]
+

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/nipy/nipy/pulls/comments/1389450232","pull_request_review_id":1724874242,"id":1389450232,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAAnNKM5S0VP4","diff_hunk":"@@ -1,20 +1,87 @@
+[project]
+name = "nipy"
+dynamic = ['version']
+license = {file = "LICENSE"}
+description  = 'A python package for analysis of neuroimaging data'
+readme = 'README.rst'
+classifiers = ["Development Status :: 3 - Alpha",
+               "Environment :: Console",
+               "Intended Audience :: Science/Research",
+               "License :: OSI Approved :: BSD License",
+               "Operating System :: OS Independent",
+               "Programming Language :: Python",
+               "Topic :: Scientific/Engineering"]
+dependencies = [
+    'numpy>=1.22',
+    'scipy>=1.8',
+    'nibabel>=3.2',
+    'sympy>=1.9',
+    'transforms3d'
+]
+
+[[project.maintainers]]
+name = 'nipy developers'
+email = 'neuroimaging@python.org'
+
+[project.urls]
+homepage = 'https://nipy.org/nipy'
+documentation = 'http://nipy.org/nipy/documentation.html'
+source = 'https://github.com/nipy/nipy'
+download = 'https://pypi.org/project/nipy/#files'
+tracker = 'https://github.com/nipy/nipy/issues'
+
+[project.optional-dependencies]
+build = [
+    'meson-python>=0.13',
+    'wheel',
+    'setuptools>=67',
+    'ninja',
+    'Cython>=3',
+    'numpy>=1.22',
+    'spin==0.3',
+    'build',
+]
+default = [
+    'numpy>=1.22',
